claims.claim: Thermal runaway (TR) is prevented by applying liquid nitrogen (LN) before the battery surface temperature reaches the critical TR suppression temperature (Ttr,cs).
claims.quote: TR can be prevented by applying LN before the critical TR suppression temperature is reached (Ttr,cs). The threshold values range from 270.2 ± 0.7 ◦C to 170.2 ± 1.1 ◦C for SOC of 25%–100%.

claims.claim: When the battery surface temperature (Tw) exceeds the critical TR suppression temperature (Ttr,cs), thermal runaway is unavoidable even during the application of liquid nitrogen.
claims.quote: When the surface temperature of the battery (Tw) exceeds Ttr,cs, TR is unavoidable even during the application of LN.

claims.claim: The delaying effect of liquid nitrogen on thermal runaway onset time is weakened as the battery surface temperature (Tw) increases toward the triggering temperature (Ttr).
claims.quote: for all SOC, when Tw is closer to the triggering temperature (Ttr), the delayed time is shorter, indicating the delaying effect of LN is weakened with increasing Tw.

claims.claim: Only 29.3 g of liquid nitrogen reduces the temperature of a 9.24 Wh (100% SOC) 18650 battery from approximately 700°C to less than 100°C within 80 seconds.
claims.quote: only 29.3 g of LN can reduce the temperature of 9.24 Wh (2.2 mAh × 4.2 V) battery from about 700 ◦C to no more than 100 ◦C within 80s, indicating that a very small of liquid nitrogen can control the triggering battery to a relatively safe level.

claims.claim: The effective heat utilization efficiency (εe) of liquid nitrogen decreases from 80% to 37.8% as the mass of LN sprayed on the battery (mLN) increases from 5.86 g to 29.3 g, indicating cooling efficiency decreases with increasing LN application duration.
claims.quote: The value of εe decreases from 80% to 37.8% when mLN rises from 5.86 to 29.3 g, implying that the cooling efficiency of LN gradually decreases with increasing mLN.

claims.claim: The calculated average cooling rate (cave) of liquid nitrogen decreases from 10.3°C/s to 2.84°C/s as the battery surface temperature (Tw) decreases from 800°C to 0°C, indicating that the cooling effect of LN weakens with decreasing Tw.
claims.quote: the cave decreases from 10.3 ◦C/s to 2.84 ◦C/s when the surface temperature reduces from 800 ◦C to 0 ◦C, indicating that the cooling effect of LN is weakened with decreasing Tw.

claims.claim: When the thermal conductivity of the composite phase change firewall substrate increases from 0.036 W/(m·K) to 0.046 W/(m·K), the peak temperature of the front surface of Cell2 rises from 136°C to 175°C (a relative increase of 22.2%), but thermal runaway zero-spreading is still achieved.
claims.quote: When the thermal conductivity of the base material increases from 0.036 W/(m⋅K) to 0.046 W/(m⋅K), although the peak temperature on the front surface of Cell2 rises from 136 ◦C to 175 ◦C (with a relative increase of 22.2 %), the module still maintains the zero-propagation effect of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: When the thermal conductivity of the composite phase change firewall substrate increases to 0.051 W/(m·K), Cell2 triggers thermal runaway, indicating a critical threshold between 0.046 W/(m·K) and 0.051 W/(m·K) above which the insulation layer fails.
claims.quote: When the thermal conductivity of the base material further increases to 0.051 W/(m⋅K), Cell2 triggers thermal runaway, which then propagates within the module. This indicates that there is a critical value of the base material's thermal conductivity between 0.046 and 0.051 W/(m⋅K): when the thermal conductivity is lower than this critical value, the composite phase change thermal insulation layer can effectively prevent the propagation of thermal runaway; once it exceeds this critical value, the insulation layer fails, and Cell 2 will trigger thermal runaway.

claims.claim: When the PCM adsorption capacity in the composite phase change firewall decreases from 100% to 50%, the peak temperature of the front surface of Cell2 rises from 137°C to 156°C (a relative increase of 13.9%), but thermal runaway zero-spreading is still achieved.
claims.quote: When the PCM adsorption capacity in the phase change thermal insulation layer decreases from 100 % to 50 %, although the peak temperature on the front surface of Cell2 rises from 137 ◦C to 156 ◦C (with a relative increase of 13.9 %), the zero-propagation effect of thermal runaway is still achieved.

claims.claim: When the PCM adsorption capacity in the composite phase change firewall decreases to 40%, Cell2 triggers thermal runaway, indicating a critical threshold between 40% and 50% below which the firewall no longer prevents thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: When the PCM adsorption capacity further decreases to 40 %, Cell2 triggers thermal runaway. This indicates that there is a critical value of PCM adsorption capacity between 40 % and 50 %: when the PCM adsorption capacity is lower than this critical value, the composite phase change thermal insulation layer no longer has the effect of preventing the propagation of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: With increasing substrate thermal conductivity of the composite phase change firewall, the TR propagation time interval D1,2 decreases in a gradient, with D1,2 shrinking from 2900 s at 0.051 W/(m·K) to 2372 s at 0.056 W/(m·K).
claims.quote: When the thermal conductivity of the substrate increases to 0.051 W/(m⋅K), the TR propagation time interval D1,2 of the first and second batteries is 2900 s. When the thermal conductivity of the substrate further increases to 0.056 W/(m⋅K), D1,2 shrinks to 2372 s. With the increase of the thermal conductivity of the substrate material, D1,2 decreases in a gradient.

claims.claim: Water mist containing Tween 20 achieved a heat dissipation of 12.46 ± 0.20 kJ, which is 23.6% higher than the test with pure water.
claims.quote: The heat dissipation of Tween 20 is 12.46 ± 0.20 kJ, which exhibits the best cooling effect, and it is increased by 23.6% compared with the test with pure water.

claims.claim: The maximum heat dissipation rate of water mist is 308.38 W, which is only 3.54% of the heat generation rate during thermal runaway, making it insufficient to restrain the rapid temperature rise of the cell during TR.
claims.quote: Thus, the max Pw is about 308.38 W, which is only 3.54% of Pg. Hence, it is hard for water mist to restrain the rapid temperature rise of the cell during TR.

claims.claim: Water mist containing FS-51 inhibited heat transfer compared to pure water, with the lowest average cooling rate of T2 at 5.47 ± 0.33 °C/s versus 6.60 ± 0.37 °C/s for pure water.
claims.quote: Different from other additives, FS-51 has the lowest cooling rates of T1 and T2, which indicates that water mist containing FS-51 could not provide a better cooling effect, but would inhibit the cooling of pure water.

claims.claim: The binary mixture of Tween 20 and 1-heptanol at any volume ratio did not exhibit better cooling performance than either single additive alone.
claims.quote: Hence, no matter what the volume ratio was mixed, the binary mixture of Tween 20 and 1-heptanol did not exhibit a better cooling performance than a single additive.

claims.claim: FS-51, despite having the lowest surface tension (15.0 mN/m), inhibited cooling due to its highest foamability with an expansion ratio of 49.4%, which was 20.6% higher than that of Tween 20.
claims.quote: FS-51 showed the best performance among other additives, and its expansion ratio was 49.4%, which was 20.6% higher than that of Tween 20... The FS-51 had a low surface tension, which facilitated cooling, but the ultimate effect was to inhibit cooling due to foamability.
